title: Flooded-road turn-around rule
summary: Make turning around the default when water covers a road, bridge, underpass, ford, or unknown surface.
content:
Make turning around the default when water covers a road, bridge, underpass, ford, or unknown surface.

Practical sequence: Do not walk or drive into moving or depth-unknown water; choose higher ground and a verified alternative route. Confirm the reason for movement, official instruction, destination, primary route, fallback route, weather, daylight, and transport limits for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”. Mark hazards such as fire, smoke, floodwater, damaged bridges, unstable slopes, crowds, poor visibility, and restricted areas for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”. Match pace, load, rest, assistance, and transport to children, older adults, disability, illness, animals, and the slowest safe member for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”. Leave a departure time, route, destination, group list, check-in schedule, and missed-contact rule with a trusted person for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”. Stop at planned checkpoints, compare real conditions with the map, and turn back or divert before the route becomes trapping or irreversible for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”. Safety limits: Official instructions and emergency services override “Flooded-road turn-around rule”; do not delay evacuation or urgent help to complete the card. Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route in “Flooded-road turn-around rule”. Do not split the group without a written destination, communication rule, responsible adults, and reunification plan in “Flooded-road turn-around rule”. Reduced fallback: If “paper map and pencil for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”” is unavailable, use only a clean, labelled substitute that performs the same function; otherwise mark the missing capability and pause that step.
materials: paper map and pencil for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”
official alert and route information for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”
contact and meeting-point card for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”
light, water, and essential medicine for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”
mobility and visibility aids for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”
steps: Do not walk or drive into moving or depth-unknown water; choose higher ground and a verified alternative route.
Confirm the reason for movement, official instruction, destination, primary route, fallback route, weather, daylight, and transport limits for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.
Mark hazards such as fire, smoke, floodwater, damaged bridges, unstable slopes, crowds, poor visibility, and restricted areas for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.
Match pace, load, rest, assistance, and transport to children, older adults, disability, illness, animals, and the slowest safe member for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.
Leave a departure time, route, destination, group list, check-in schedule, and missed-contact rule with a trusted person for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.
Stop at planned checkpoints, compare real conditions with the map, and turn back or divert before the route becomes trapping or irreversible for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.
warnings: Official instructions and emergency services override “Flooded-road turn-around rule”; do not delay evacuation or urgent help to complete the card.
Never enter moving floodwater, wildfire closure, smoke-obscured road, unstable slope, damaged structure, or officially restricted route in “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.
Do not split the group without a written destination, communication rule, responsible adults, and reunification plan in “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.
common_mistakes: Skipping the opening control makes “Flooded-road turn-around rule” unreliable: Do not walk or drive into moving or depth-unknown water; choose higher ground and a verified alternative route.

Treating the result as safe while ignoring this limit is a serious error: Official instructions and emergency services override “Flooded-road turn-around rule”; do not delay evacuation or urgent help to complete the card.

Failing to document the final handover action leaves the next person without a trustworthy starting point: Stop at planned checkpoints, compare real conditions with the map, and turn back or divert before the route becomes trapping or irreversible for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.
alternatives: If “paper map and pencil for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”” is unavailable, use only a clean, labelled substitute that performs the same function; otherwise mark the missing capability and pause that step.

For a reduced version of “Flooded-road turn-around rule”, complete the first two verified actions—Do not walk or drive into moving or depth-unknown water; choose higher ground and a verified alternative route. Confirm the reason for movement, official instruction, destination, primary route, fallback route, weather, daylight, and transport limits for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”.—then stop at the first uncertainty and request appropriate help.
failure_signs: Stop and reassess “Flooded-road turn-around rule” when the route loses a safe exit, visibility, communications, daylight margin, water margin, or physical capacity.
Stop and reassess “Flooded-road turn-around rule” when a member is missing, distressed, injured, exhausted, confused, or unable to continue safely.
when_not_to_use: Do not continue “Flooded-road turn-around rule” while the area has smoke, gas odour, rising water, unstable walls or roof, falling objects, blocked exits, extreme heat or cold, or an evacuation order.

This card is not authorization to re-enter a restricted structure, shelter in a flood-prone basement, use flame near fuel, or ignore an official evacuation route. Topic-specific stop check for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”: also follow this warning: “Official instructions and emergency services override “Flooded-road turn-around rule”; do not delay evacuation or urgent help to complete the card.”.
short_term: For the first operating cycle of “Flooded-road turn-around rule”, carry out do not walk or drive into moving or depth-unknown water; choose higher ground and a verified alternative route. Follow with confirm the reason for movement, official instruction, destination, primary route, fallback route, weather, daylight, and transport limits for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”. Before repeating the procedure, verify that this failure sign is absent: Stop and reassess “Flooded-road turn-around rule” when the route loses a safe exit, visibility, communications, daylight margin, water margin, or physical capacity.
long_term: For repeated use of “Flooded-road turn-around rule”, keep “official alert and route information for “Flooded-road turn-around rule”” serviceable, rehearse the handover described in the final step, compare records with real outcomes, and revise the card whenever local conditions or official instructions change.
if_method_fails: If “Flooded-road turn-around rule” does not produce a verifiable result, stop using that result, label or isolate anything uncertain, protect people from the identified failure path, record what happened, and change to the safest official, professionally supported, or previously tested fallback.
environment_notes: Match the plan to building age, balcony and stair access, island or village isolation, wildfire vegetation, coastal exposure, heat, frost, and mobility needs. For “Flooded-road turn-around rule”, also account for the people, animals, tools, transport, communications, and qualified help that are actually available at the location.
